frames.caption: Start both pointers at the head (node 3). slow is the tortoise (steps 1), fast is the hare (steps 2).
frames.note: Two pointers walk the SAME list at different speeds: slow (tortoise) one node per step, fast (hare) two. If the list ends, fast runs off the tail (hits null) first and there is no cycle. If instead there is a loop, fast can never escape it — so once both pointers are inside the loop, fast is gaining one node on slow every step and the gap must eventually hit zero. That collision is the cycle proof. O(n) time, O(1) extra space — just the two pointers.

frames.caption: slow -> 2 (one hop), fast -> 0 (two hops). Not equal yet; the hare is closing the gap by one node.
frames.note: Advance slow by one and fast by two, then re-check fast == slow. Why a meeting is guaranteed inside a cycle: think in the loop's frame. Each step slow moves +1 and fast moves +2, so fast closes the distance to slow by exactly 1 node per step. A positive integer gap that strictly decreases by 1 every step can never skip past 0 — it lands on 0, i.e. fast catches slow. (It cannot "jump over" because the closing speed is exactly one node.) Outside a cycle this never gets the chance: fast reaches null and we would return false instead.

frames.caption: slow -> 0 (one hop), fast -> 2 (two hops). Not equal yet; the hare is closing the gap by one node.
frames.note: Advance slow by one and fast by two, then re-check fast == slow. Why a meeting is guaranteed inside a cycle: think in the loop's frame. Each step slow moves +1 and fast moves +2, so fast closes the distance to slow by exactly 1 node per step. A positive integer gap that strictly decreases by 1 every step can never skip past 0 — it lands on 0, i.e. fast catches slow. (It cannot "jump over" because the closing speed is exactly one node.) Outside a cycle this never gets the chance: fast reaches null and we would return false instead.

frames.caption: fast caught slow on node -4 — they collided inside the loop. Cycle detected: return true.
frames.note: fast == slow: the hare has lapped the tortoise and they sit on the same node. This can only happen inside a loop (on a straight list fast would have reached null), so we return true. Note we only needed two pointers and a constant amount of memory — no visited-set, no hashing, no O(n) space. (If you also wanted the cycle's entry node, you would now reset one pointer to the head and walk both at speed 1 until they meet again.)
